Part I · Analytical Geometry ¶ Lecture Topic Scope Status 01 Cartesian Geometry and Coordinates Constructing axes, scale, coordinates, graphs and Euclidean distance. Open → 02 Vectors, Bases, and Coordinate Systems Directed changes, vector operations, dot products, projection and one concrete change of basis. Open → 03 Lines and Planes in the Plane and in Space Parametric and normal descriptions, intersections, nearest points, planes, normals and distance. Open → 04 Conic Sections Through Cartesian and Polar Eyes Classical conics, polar coordinates, eccentricity and one focus--directrix formula. Planned 06 Systems of Linear Equations Intersections, elimination and solution structure. Planned 07 Determinants and Orientation Area scaling, invertibility and geometric meaning. Planned 08 Basis and Change of Coordinates Different descriptions of the same vector space. Planned Part III · Calculus ¶ Lecture Topic Scope Status 09 Functions and Their Graphs Dependence, representation and qualitative behaviour. Planned 10 Limits and Continuity Approach, approximation and local stability. Planned 11 Derivative and Local Change Secants, tangents and linear approximation. Planned 12 Integral and Accumulation Area, sums and the fundamental theorem. Planned

docs.text: Authoring and deployment ¶ Local preview ¶ python -m venv .venv source .venv/bin/activate # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate python -m pip install -r requirements.txt mkdocs serve Deploy to GitHub Pages ¶ mkdocs build --strict mkdocs gh-deploy --force --clean Configure GitHub Pages to publish from the root of the gh-pages branch. Modular lecture structure ¶ Each lecture remains one long reading page, matching the reference site, while its source is split into semantic section modules: docs/lecture-02/ ├── index.md └── sections/ ├── 01-from-points.md ├── 02-addition.md └── ... index.md assembles the modules with pymdownx.snippets . All headings, paragraphs, lists, tables, exercises, proofs and formulas are authored in Markdown. HTML is reserved for the internal controls and host elements of interactive figures. Semantic mathematical environments ¶ Use Markdown admonitions instead of hand-written HTML wrappers. Definition ¶ !!! definition "Vector norm" For $\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]$ define $$ \|\mathbf v\|=\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}. $$ Theorem ¶ !!! theorem "Projection theorem" Every vector admits an orthogonal decomposition. Long derivation ¶ !!! derivation "Eliminating the parameter" First substitute the preceding identity: $$ \begin{aligned} F(x) &=A(x)+B(x)\\ &=C(x)+D(x). \end{aligned} $$ Continue the argument in ordinary Markdown paragraphs. Collapsible long proof ¶ ??? proof "Proof" The proof may contain any number of paragraphs, lists and equations. $$ \begin{aligned} A _1 &= A_ 2 \\ &= A_3 \\ &= A_4. \end{aligned} $$ Continue until the argument is complete. $\square$ Available styled types include: definition, theorem, proof, derivation, principle, interpretation, example, problem, question, consequence, summary, warning, note Long proofs and multiline mathematics ¶ MathJax supports aligned , alignedat , gathered , cases , matrix , pmatrix , and bmatrix . A proof should normally be split into several meaningful display equations rather than one extremely wide formula. On narrow screens, display mathematics receives horizontal scrolling as a fallback. Interactive figures and fullscreen ¶ Interactive figures are the one intentional HTML exception because their buttons, sliders and data-* hooks are consumed directly by JavaScript. Keep the host markup local to the section where the figure appears: < figure class = "figure-panel jsx-panel" data-fullscreen-panel tabindex = "0" > < div class = "figure-toolbar" > < span class = "figure-title" > Figure title </ span > < button class = "icon-button" type = "button" data-fullscreen > ⛶ </ button > </ div > < div class = "figure-stage jsx-stage" > < div id = "example-board" class = "jxgbox" data-example-board ></ div > </ div > </ figure > The data-fullscreen-panel and data-fullscreen attributes activate the same enlargement behaviour as the original custom site. Do not place ordinary prose, definitions, proofs or formulas inside figure markup.

docs.text: Before coordinates ¶ A Euclidean plane already contains points, straight lines, right angles and lengths. Coordinates do not create these objects. They provide a systematic numerical description after we make several explicit choices. Available geometry Before coordinates we may construct lines through points, perpendicular lines, circles, intersections and lengths. What is missing is an origin, orientation and numerical scale. Constructing the Cartesian system ¶ The sequence below distinguishes permanent objects from temporary construction aids. Compass circles and bisectors disappear after they have produced the required mark. Open the figure full screen during a lecture and move through the construction one step at a time. Construction sequence · Cartesian plane Euclidean plane ⛶ Previous Next Play Reset Temporary construction objects are removed after each operation. The final coordinate system retains only the axes, origin, orientations and scale. Construction principle The scale is not drawn at once. A fixed compass opening is transferred successively along each axis. The interval is then refined by geometric bisection before coordinates are assigned by orthogonal projection. Assigning coordinates ¶ Let \(P\) be any point of the plane. Through \(P\) , construct perpendiculars to both axes. Their intersection points with the axes determine two signed real numbers \(x\) and \(y\) . Coordinate correspondence Once two perpendicular axes, their positive directions and a common unit are fixed, orthogonal projection establishes a one-to-one correspondence between points of the Euclidean plane and ordered pairs \((x,y)\in\mathbb{R}^2\) . Proof For every point \(P\) , the perpendicular to each axis is unique, so the two projections are uniquely determined. Conversely, for fixed real numbers \(x\) and \(y\) , the perpendiculars through the corresponding points on the axes meet in exactly one point. Therefore the correspondence is bijective. \(\square\) The coordinate formula for distance ¶ For points \(P=(x_1,y_1)\) and \(Q=(x_2,y_2)\) , introduce the auxiliary point \(R=(x_2,y_1)\) . Then \(PR\) is horizontal, \(RQ\) is vertical, and \(PQR\) is a right triangle. Distance as a right triangle Decomposition into horizontal and vertical components ⛶ Move $P$ or $Q$. The horizontal difference, vertical difference and Euclidean distance update together. The two legs have lengths \(|x_2-x_1|\) and \(|y_2-y_1|\) . Applying the Pythagorean theorem to this visible triangle gives the coordinate formula. \[ \begin{aligned} d_E(P,Q)^2 &= (x_2-x_1)^2+(y_2-y_1)^2,\\ d_E(P,Q) &= \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2+(y_2-y_1)^2}. \end{aligned} \] Interpretation The formula does not invent distance. It translates the already existing Euclidean length of the segment \(PQ\) into arithmetic performed on coordinates. How a function generates points ¶ A function is a rule that accepts an allowed input \(x\) and produces exactly one output \(y=f(x)\) . The ordered pair \((x,f(x))\) is then placed in the Cartesian plane. From an input to a point $y=2x+1$ ⛶ Linear function Inverse proportionality Choose input $x=1$ Substitute $y=2\cdot1+1$ Calculate $y=3$ Plot $(1,3)$ input $x$ = 1 Move the input slider. The substitution, output and point update together. Previously generated points remain faintly visible. Function test For each allowed value of \(x\) , a function gives one and only one value of \(y\) . The graph is the collection of all points \((x,f(x))\) . When one input gives more than one point ¶ Not every geometric equation defines \(y\) as a function of \(x\) . For the circle \[ x^2+y^2=r^2, \] fixing \(x\) may produce two values of \(y\) : \[ y=\pm\sqrt{r^2-x^2}. \] A circle as a relation $x^2+y^2=9$ ⛶ $x=1$ $y=\pm\sqrt{8}$ two points radius $r$ = 3 chosen $x$ = 1 The vertical line at the chosen $x$ meets the circle in zero, one or two points. Two intersections mean that the circle is not the graph of a single-valued function $y=f(x)$. Curves described by one parameter ¶ A parametric curve uses one number \(t\) to produce both coordinates at once: \[ t\longmapsto (x(t),y(t)). \] As \(t\) changes, the point moves. The complete path traced by that moving point is the curve. A circle is therefore still one-dimensional: one parameter is enough to move around it. One parameter, one moving point $x(t)=3\cos t,\ y(t)=3\sin t$ $t=0$, point $(3,0)$ ⛶ Circle Ellipse Parabola parameter $t$ = 0.00 Move $t$. The point changes both coordinates simultaneously and travels along the curve. Intersections and regions defined by conditions ¶ Two equations impose two conditions. Points satisfying both equations are their intersection points. Replacing equations by inequalities selects whole regions of the plane. Several conditions at once $y\ge x^2-2$ and $y\le x+2$ The shaded points lie above the parabola and below the line. ⛶ Parabola and line Three linear boundaries Disk and half-plane Highlighted boundary points satisfy two equations simultaneously. Every shaded point satisfies all displayed inequalities. Exercises ¶ The first ten exercises reinforce the basic computational procedures of the lecture. The next ten require explanation, modelling and argument rather than only substitution into a formula. Download exercises as Markdown Technical and computational practice ¶ Exercises 1–10: plot, substitute, calculate and verify. Plot the points \(A=(-3,2)\) , \(B=(4,-1)\) , \(C=(0,5)\) and \(D=(-2,-4)\) . State the quadrant of each point that does not lie on an axis. For \(P=(-2,3)\) and \(Q=(4,-5)\) , compute the horizontal difference, the vertical difference and the Euclidean distance \(d_E(P,Q)\) . For \(y=2x+1\) , calculate \(y\) for \(x=-3,-1,0,2,4\) , and write the five corresponding points of the graph. For \(y=\frac{6}{x}\) , calculate the points corresponding to \(x=-6,-3,-1,1,2,6\) . Indicate which inputs are not allowed. The circle \(x^2+y^2=25\) is intersected by the vertical lines \(x=0\) , \(x=3\) , \(x=5\) and \(x=6\) . For each line, find all real values of \(y\) and state how many intersection points occur. Decide whether \((0,0)\) , \((4,0)\) , \((2,3)\) , \((-1,4)\) and \((5,-2)\) lie inside, on, or outside the disk \(x^2+y^2\le16\) . For \(x(t)=3\cos t\) , \(y(t)=3\sin t\) , compute the point for \(t=0,\frac{\pi}{2},\pi,\frac{3\pi}{2},2\pi\) . For \(x(t)=t\) , \(y(t)=t^2-2\) , compute the points corresponding to \(t=-2,-1,0,1,2\) . Verify that every computed point satisfies \(y=x^2-2\) . Find all intersection points of \(y=x^2-2\) and \(y=x+2\) . Give exact coordinates and decimal approximations. For each of \((0,0)\) , \((0,3)\) , \((2,3)\) , \((-2,0)\) and \((1,2)\) , decide whether it belongs to the region \(y\ge x^2-2\) , \(y\le x+2\) . Problems requiring explanation and argument ¶ Exercises 11–20: explain why, derive a rule, or justify a geometric conclusion. A Cartesian coordinate system requires an origin, two perpendicular axes, positive directions and a unit length. Explain what information is lost if any one of these choices is omitted. Give a concrete example in each case. Prove from the coordinate formula that \(d_E(P,Q)=d_E(Q,P)\) . Then show that translating both points by the same vector does not change their distance. The equation \(x^2+y^2=9\) does not define \(y\) as a function of \(x\) on the whole circle. Explain why. Find two natural restrictions that do define functions and write their formulas. For \(y=\frac{4}{x}\) , explain why the graph has two branches, never meets either coordinate axis, and is symmetric with respect to the origin. The parametrization \(x(t)=3\cos t\) , \(y(t)=3\sin t\) uses one parameter to describe a circle. Explain why this makes the circle one-dimensional although it lies in a plane. Is the parameter value associated with a point unique? For \(P=(p_1,p_2)\) and \(Q=(q_1,q_2)\) , construct a parametrization of the segment from \(P\) to \(Q\) using \(t\in[0,1]\) . Explain the roles of \(t=0\) , \(t=1\) and the intermediate values. Determine exactly for which values of \(x\) there exist points satisfying both \(y\ge x^2-2\) and \(y\le x+2\) . Use the boundary intersections and explain why the region is bounded. For which values of \(m\) and \(b\) does \(y=mx+b\) meet \(y=x^2\) in exactly one point? Derive a condition and interpret it geometrically. Starting from the distance formula, derive the equation \((x-h)^2+(y-k)^2=r^2\) of a circle with centre \((h,k)\) and radius \(r>0\) . Explain why replacing equality by \(\le\) gives the disk. For the upper half-disk \(x^2+y^2\le9\) , \(y\ge0\) and the region \(y\ge x^2-2\) , \(y\le x+2\) , identify the boundary curves and the points satisfying two boundary equations. Decide whether each region is convex and justify your answer.

docs.text: Lecture 1 — Cartesian Geometry and Coordinates ¶ A. Technical and computational practice ¶ Plot the points [ A=(-3,2),\quad B=(4,-1),\quad C=(0,5),\quad D=(-2,-4). ] State the quadrant of each point that does not lie on an axis. For [ P=(-2,3),\qquad Q=(4,-5), ] compute the horizontal difference, the vertical difference and the Euclidean distance \(d_E(P,Q)\) . For the function [ y=2x+1, ] calculate \(y\) for \(x=-3,-1,0,2,4\) , and write the five corresponding points of its graph. For the inverse proportionality [ y=\frac{6}{x}, ] calculate the points corresponding to \(x=-6,-3,-1,1,2,6\) . Indicate which inputs are not allowed. The circle [ x^2+y^2=25 ] is intersected by the vertical lines \(x=0\) , \(x=3\) , \(x=5\) and \(x=6\) . For each line, find all real values of \(y\) and state how many intersection points occur. Decide whether each point lies inside, on, or outside the disk [ x^2+y^2\le 16: ] [ (0,0),\quad (4,0),\quad (2,3),\quad (-1,4),\quad (5,-2). ] A circle is parametrized by [ x(t)=3\cos t,\qquad y(t)=3\sin t. ] Compute the point for [ t=0,\quad \frac{\pi}{2},\quad \pi,\quad \frac{3\pi}{2},\quad 2\pi. ] For the parametric parabola [ x(t)=t,\qquad y(t)=t^2-2, ] compute the points corresponding to \(t=-2,-1,0,1,2\) . Verify directly that every computed point satisfies \(y=x^2-2\) . Find all intersection points of [ y=x^2-2 ] and [ y=x+2. ] Give exact coordinates and decimal approximations. For each point below, decide whether it belongs to the region [ y\ge x^2-2, \qquad y\le x+2: ] [ (0,0),\quad (0,3),\quad (2,3),\quad (-2,0),\quad (1,2). ] B. Problems requiring explanation and argument ¶ A Cartesian coordinate system requires an origin, two perpendicular axes, positive directions and a unit length. Explain what information is lost if any one of these choices is omitted. Give a concrete example in each case. Prove directly from the coordinate formula that [ d_E(P,Q)=d_E(Q,P). ] Then show that translating both points by the same vector does not change their distance. The equation [ x^2+y^2=9 ] does not define \(y\) as a function of \(x\) on the whole circle. Explain why. Find two natural restrictions of the circle that do define functions and write their formulas. Consider [ y=\frac{4}{x}. ] Explain, without drawing a complete table of values, why its graph has two branches, why it never meets either coordinate axis, and why it is symmetric with respect to the origin. The parametrization [ x(t)=3\cos t,\qquad y(t)=3\sin t ] uses one real parameter to describe a circle. Explain why this shows that the circle is a one-dimensional object even though it lies in a two-dimensional plane. Is the parameter value associated with a point unique? Justify your answer. Let [ P=(p_1,p_2),\qquad Q=(q_1,q_2). ] Construct a parametrization of the line segment from \(P\) to \(Q\) using a parameter \(t\in[0,1]\) . Explain why the endpoints occur at \(t=0\) and \(t=1\) , and why every intermediate value gives a point on the segment. Determine exactly for which values of \(x\) there exist points satisfying both [ y\ge x^2-2 ] and [ y\le x+2. ] Use the boundary intersections to justify your answer, and explain why the resulting region is bounded. For which values of \(m\) and \(b\) does the line [ y=mx+b ] meet the parabola [ y=x^2 ] in exactly one point? Derive a condition on \(m\) and \(b\) , and interpret the result geometrically. A circle has centre \((h,k)\) and radius \(r>0\) . Starting from the distance formula, derive its equation [ (x-h)^2+(y-k)^2=r^2. ] Explain why replacing equality by \(\le\) produces the entire disk. Consider the two regions [ A={(x,y):x^2+y^2\le 9,\ y\ge0} ] and [ B={(x,y):y\ge x^2-2,\ y\le x+2}. ] For each region, explain which curves form its boundary and which boundary points satisfy two equations simultaneously. Then decide whether the region appears convex and justify your conclusion geometrically or algebraically.

docs.text:
From Cartesian points to free vectors ¶ A point of the Cartesian plane is an ordered pair of real numbers. Let \[ P=(p_1,p_2),\qquad Q=(q_1,q_2). \] Before vectors are introduced, we first define several arithmetic operations on such points. These definitions are possible because a point of the Cartesian plane is represented by an ordered pair of numbers. Arithmetic operations on Cartesian points ¶ The point \[ O=(0,0) \] plays the role of zero. Addition of points is defined coordinatewise: \[  oxed{P+Q=(p_1+q_1,\,p_2+q_2)}. \] The point opposite to \(P\) is \[  oxed{-P=(-p_1,-p_2)}, \] so that \[ P+(-P)=O. \] Subtraction of points is defined by adding the opposite point: \[  oxed{Q-P=Q+(-P)=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2)}. \] For a real number \(\lambda\) , multiplication of a point by a number is also defined coordinatewise: \[  oxed{\lambda P=(\lambda p_1,\,\lambda p_2)}. \] At this stage these are arithmetic operations on ordered pairs. They are useful computational rules, but their geometric meaning has not yet been established. The first expression to acquire such a meaning will be the difference \(Q-P\) . Arithmetic on Cartesian points Move $P$ and $Q$, then compare the four coordinatewise operations. ⛶ Addition Opposite point Subtraction Scalar multiplication $\lambda$ = 1.5 The figure visualises arithmetic on ordered pairs. The interpretation of $Q-P$ as a directed displacement is introduced immediately afterwards. An ordered pair of points determines an anchored vector ¶ The ordered pair \((P,Q)\) determines an arrow beginning at \(P\) and ending at \(Q\) . We denote it by \[ \overrightarrow{PQ} \] and call it an anchored vector . The order matters: \(P\) is its initial point and \(Q\) is its endpoint. An anchored vector and its coordinate changes The horizontal and vertical changes are $q_1-p_1$ and $q_2-p_2$. ⛶ The arrow begins at $P$ and ends at $Q$; its coordinates record the two coordinate changes. The point difference is \[ Q-P=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2). \] The two numbers \(q_1-p_1\) and \(q_2-p_2\) are, by definition, the coordinates of the anchored vector \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) . Round brackets remain reserved for Cartesian points. Its length is the distance between the two points: \[  oxed{|\overrightarrow{PQ}|=d_E(P,Q)}. \] Reversing the order reverses the arrow and changes the signs of both coordinates. The vector \(\overrightarrow{QP}\) therefore has coordinates \[ p_1-q_1,\qquad p_2-q_2. \] When do arrows drawn in different places represent the same vector? ¶ We first define equality geometrically. Consider two anchored vectors \[ \overrightarrow{PQ}\qquad	ext{and}\qquad\overrightarrow{P'Q'}. \] They are called equal when the midpoint of the pair \(P,Q'\) is also the midpoint of the pair \(Q,P'\) . Equivalently, the four points form a parallelogram in which the two arrows are opposite directed sides. The midpoint condition is \[  rac12(P+Q')= rac12(Q+P'). \] Rearranging this equality gives \[ Q-P=Q'-P'. \] We have therefore obtained the coordinate criterion for the geometric relation: \[  oxed{\overrightarrow{PQ}=\overrightarrow{P'Q'}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad Q-P=Q'-P'}. \] Thus two anchored vectors are equal precisely when their corresponding coordinates are equal. Their initial points may be different. Free vectors ¶ Equality divides all anchored vectors into disjoint classes. Each class contains all arrows representing one and the same directed change. Such a class is called a free vector . If \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) is one representative of a free vector \(\mathbf v\) , we write \[  oxed{\mathbf v=[\overrightarrow{PQ}]}. \] Here the square brackets denote the equivalence class of all anchored vectors equal to \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) . If the common coordinates of these representatives are \(v_1,v_2\) , then the same free vector is written \[  oxed{\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]}. \] Thus round brackets distinguish points from vectors: \[ V=(v_1,v_2)\qquad	ext{is a point, whereas}\qquad \mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\qquad	ext{is a free vector}. \] Since \[ Q-P=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2) \] is a Cartesian point, placing square brackets around this point means the free vector with the same coordinate entries: \[  oxed{[\overrightarrow{PQ}]=[Q-P]=[q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2]}. \] The outer square brackets change the type of object: \((q_1-p_1,q_2-p_2)\) is a point, while \([q_1-p_1,q_2-p_2]\) is a free vector. A particularly convenient representative begins at the origin. If \[ O=(0,0),\qquad V=(v_1,v_2), \] then \[ \mathbf v=[\overrightarrow{OV}]=[V]=[v_1,v_2]. \] This does not identify the point \(V\) with the vector \(\mathbf v\) ; it gives a canonical representative of the free vector. Order of construction Only now, after free vectors have been constructed, do we define operations on them. Each operation will first be described using representatives and then written in coordinates. Addition, opposite vectors, and subtraction ¶ Why an operation of addition is needed ¶ Suppose that we begin at a point \(P\) , move to a point \(Q\) , and then move from \(Q\) to a point \(R\) . These are two consecutive geometric changes: \[ P\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow R. \] The first change is represented by the anchored vector \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) and the second by \(\overrightarrow{QR}\) . But the same journey also produces one direct change from the initial point \(P\) to the final point \(R\) , represented by \(\overrightarrow{PR}\) . The problem Given the two free vectors represented by \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) and \(\overrightarrow{QR}\) , construct one free vector that represents their combined effect. This geometric problem leads to the definition of vector addition. Two consecutive changes and one direct change Move $P$, $Q$, or $R$ and compare the route $P	o Q	o R$ with the direct change $P	o R$. ⛶ The two-step motion and the direct motion have the same initial and final points. The direct anchored vector is therefore the natural candidate for the combined free vector. From two successive displacements to vector addition ¶ Let the free vector represented by \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) have coordinates \[ \mathbf a=[a_1,a_2], \] and let the free vector represented by \(\overrightarrow{QR}\) have coordinates \[ \mathbf b=[b_1,b_2]. \] Because \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) has coordinate change \((a_1,a_2)\) , \[ Q=P+(a_1,a_2). \] Because \(\overrightarrow{QR}\) has coordinate change \((b_1,b_2)\) , \[ R=Q+(b_1,b_2). \] Now substitute the expression for the point \(Q\) into the formula for the point \(R\) : \[  egin{aligned} R &=Q+(b_1,b_2)\ &= igl(P+(a_1,a_2) igr)+(b_1,b_2). \end{aligned} \] At this point we are still using the coordinatewise addition of Cartesian points introduced earlier. By associativity, \[ R=P+ igl((a_1,a_2)+(b_1,b_2) igr). \] Adding the two ordered pairs coordinatewise gives \[ (a_1,a_2)+(b_1,b_2)=(a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2), \] and therefore \[  oxed{R=P+(a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2).} \] Hence the direct anchored vector \(\overrightarrow{PR}\) has coordinates \[ [\overrightarrow{PR}]=[a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2]. \] These coordinates depend only on the two free vectors \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\) , not on the particular starting point \(P\) . We therefore define \[  oxed{\mathbf a+\mathbf b=[\overrightarrow{PR}]=[a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2].} \] The parallelogram rule and commutativity ¶ The same sum can be constructed in the opposite order. Placing a representative of \(\mathbf b\) first and then one of \(\mathbf a\) gives the same endpoint. Hence \[  oxed{\mathbf a+\mathbf b=\mathbf b+\mathbf a.} \] Opposite vectors ¶ For every vector \(\mathbf a=[a_1,a_2]\) there is an opposite vector \[  oxed{-\mathbf a=[-a_1,-a_2]}, \] characterised by \[ \mathbf a+(-\mathbf a)=\mathbf0. \] Geometrically it has the same length and lies on the same direction line as \(\mathbf a\) , but its orientation is reversed. Subtraction as addition of the opposite vector ¶ Opposite vector and subtraction First reverse $\mathbf b$, then add $-\mathbf b$ to $\mathbf a$. ⛶ Construct $-\mathbf b$ Add $\mathbf a+(-\mathbf b)$ Check the missing vector The opposite vector reverses orientation without changing length. Subtraction is then ordinary vector addition with this reversed vector. Subtraction is defined by adding the opposite vector: \[  oxed{\mathbf a-\mathbf b=\mathbf a+(-\mathbf b)=[a_1-b_1,\,a_2-b_2].} \] It is the missing vector in the equation \[ (\mathbf a-\mathbf b)+\mathbf b=\mathbf a. \] Points and vectors For any two points \(P\) and \(Q\) , \[ [\overrightarrow{PQ}]=[Q-P]. \] The subtraction is performed on their coordinate descriptions and the result is interpreted as the free vector represented by the arrow from \(P\) to \(Q\) . Parallel vectors, multiplication by a number, and linear combinations ¶ Before multiplying a vector by a number, we first identify the geometric relation that this operation is meant to preserve. Parallel vectors from length equalities Two nonzero free vectors \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\) have the same direction and the same orientation when placing them successively produces no shortening: \[ |\mathbf a+\mathbf b|=|\mathbf a|+|\mathbf b|. \] They have the same direction and opposite orientations when \[ |\mathbf a-\mathbf b|=|\mathbf a|+|\mathbf b|. \] In both cases the vectors are called parallel. For \(\mathbf a=[a_1,a_2]\) and \(\mathbf b=[b_1,b_2]\) , equality in the triangle inequality gives the coordinate criterion \[  oxed{\mathbf a\parallel\mathbf b\iff \lambda\mathbf a=\mu\mathbf b} \] for some real numbers \(\lambda\) and \(\mu\) that are not both zero. In the plane this may also be written as \[  oxed{a_1b_2-a_2b_1=0.} \] If \(\lambda\) and \(\mu\) have the same sign, the orientations agree; if their signs are opposite, the orientations are opposite. Multiplication by a real number ¶ If \(t>0\) , then \(t\mathbf a\) is parallel to \(\mathbf a\) , has the same orientation, and \[ |t\mathbf a|=t|\mathbf a|. \] If \(t<0\) , the orientation is reversed and \[ |t\mathbf a|=|t|\,|\mathbf a|. \] For \(t=0\) , the result is the zero vector. In coordinates, \[  oxed{t[a_1,a_2]=[ta_1,ta_2].} \] Scaling and combining vectors $2\mathbf u-\mathbf v$ ⛶ $a$ = 2 $b$ = -1 The two scaled displacements are added head to tail to form $a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v$. Scalar multiplication is compatible with addition: \[  oxed{t(\mathbf a+\mathbf b)=t\mathbf a+t\mathbf b}, \qquad  oxed{(s+t)\mathbf a=s\mathbf a+t\mathbf a}. \] These identities follow from coordinatewise arithmetic, while geometrically they say that scaling commutes with combining directed changes. Linear combinations: what addition and scaling can build ¶ Linear combination Given vectors \(\mathbf u,\mathbf v\) and real numbers \(a,b\) , the vector \[  oxed{a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v} \] is called a linear combination of \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) . First scale \(\mathbf u\) by \(a\) and \(\mathbf v\) by \(b\) , then add the resulting displacements. A linear combination is therefore not a new operation. One direction versus two directions A single nonzero vector generates only the line \[ \{\lambda\mathbf v:\lambda\in\mathbb R\}. \] Two parallel vectors still generate one line. Two nonparallel vectors provide two independent directions and can generate the whole plane. Why nonparallel directions reach the plane If \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) are parallel, every multiple of either vector lies on the same line, so every sum \(a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v\) remains on that line. If they are not parallel, the parallelogram construction decomposes any target displacement into one component along each direction. The two coefficients record those two signed motions. Basis theorem in the plane Two nonparallel vectors \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) determine every vector in the plane uniquely in the form \[  oxed{\mathbf w=a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v}. \] A basis is precisely such a pair of independent directions, and basis coordinates are the coefficients in this reconstruction. How much motion and in which direction? ¶ The coordinate pair of a vector records two changes, but it does not immediately answer two geometric questions: Two questions carried by every nonzero vector How long is the displacement? In which direction does it point? Let \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) be drawn from the origin. Its endpoint, together with its horizontal and vertical coordinate changes, forms a right triangle. The legs have lengths \(|v_1|\) and \(|v_2|\) , while the vector itself is the hypotenuse. A vector as a right triangle $\mathbf v=[4,3]$ ⛶ The vector, its two coordinate changes, and its inclination are displayed simultaneously. Length from the Pythagorean theorem \[ \|\mathbf v\|^2=v_1^2+v_2^2. \] The length is nonnegative, so \[  oxed{\|\mathbf v\|=\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}}. \] Euclidean norm The Euclidean length, or norm, of \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) is \[  oxed{\|\mathbf v\|=\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}}. \] The double bars distinguish the vector from the number measuring its length. Basic consequences \[  oxed{\|\mathbf0\|=0}, \qquad  oxed{\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\implies\|\mathbf v\|>0}. \] For points \(A\) and \(B\) , \[  oxed{d_E(A,B)=\|B-A\|}. \] The vector \(B-A\) records how to move from \(A\) to \(B\) , while its norm records the Euclidean distance travelled. The same object therefore carries both directional and metric information. Separating magnitude from direction ¶ Assume \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) and let \(\alpha\) be its angle of inclination measured from the positive \(x\) -axis. Recovering the coordinates The right triangle gives \[ \cos\alpha= rac{v_1}{\|\mathbf v\|}, \qquad \sin\alpha= rac{v_2}{\|\mathbf v\|}. \] Therefore \[ v_1=\|\mathbf v\|\cos\alpha, \qquad v_2=\|\mathbf v\|\sin\alpha. \] Magnitude-direction decomposition Every nonzero vector admits the decomposition \[  oxed{\mathbf v=\|\mathbf v\|[\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]}. \] The vector \([\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]\) has length one. The two pieces of a vector \(\|\mathbf v\|\) says how much displacement occurs; \([\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]\) says in which direction it occurs. Thus every nonzero vector is a positive magnitude multiplied by a unit direction. One angle calculation, two geometric tests ¶ Take two arbitrary nonzero vectors \[ \mathbf u=[u_1,u_2],\qquad \mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]. \] Let their angles of inclination be \(\alpha\) and \( eta\) . Then \[ \mathbf u=\|\mathbf u\|[\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha], \qquad \mathbf v=\|\mathbf v\|[\cos eta,\sin eta]. \] One comparison controls both tests The directed turn from \(\mathbf u\) to \(\mathbf v\) is \( eta-\alpha\) . Its sine detects parallel directions, while its cosine detects perpendicular directions. Two vectors, two coordinate tests Move the endpoints of $\mathbf u$ and $\mathbf v$. ⛶ The same angle difference produces both coordinate tests. Parallel directions arise from the sine of the angle difference ¶ Parallelism from \(\sin( eta-\alpha)\) Two nonzero vectors determine the same unoriented direction precisely when \[  eta-\alpha\equiv0\pmod\pi, \] equivalently when \(\sin( eta-\alpha)=0\) . Using \[ \sin( eta-\alpha)=\sin eta\cos\alpha-\cos eta\sin\alpha \] and substituting the coordinate expressions for the four trigonometric terms gives \[ \sin( eta-\alpha)= rac{u_1v_2-u_2v_1}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}. \] The denominator is nonzero, so the sine vanishes exactly when the numerator vanishes. Parallelism test \[  oxed{\mathbf u\parallel\mathbf v\iff u_1v_2-u_2v_1=0}. \] Equivalently, \[  oxed{\mathbf u\parallel\mathbf v\iff \mathbf u=\lambda\mathbf v	ext{ for some }\lambda
eq0}. \] Why the determinant condition gives a scalar multiple If \(v_1
eq0\) , set \(\lambda=u_1/v_1\) . The equality \(u_1v_2-u_2v_1=0\) then gives \(u_2=\lambda v_2\) , hence \(\mathbf u=\lambda\mathbf v\) . If \(v_1=0\) , then \(v_2
eq0\) and the same argument uses \(\lambda=u_2/v_2\) . The quantity \(u_1v_2-u_2v_1\) measures whether the two coordinate directions span genuine area. It is zero exactly when both vectors lie on one line. The cosine of the same difference reveals perpendicularity ¶ Perpendicularity from \(\cos( eta-\alpha)\) The vectors are perpendicular precisely when \[  eta-\alpha\equiv rac\pi2\pmod\pi, \] equivalently when \(\cos( eta-\alpha)=0\) . The identity \[ \cos( eta-\alpha)=\cos eta\cos\alpha+\sin eta\sin\alpha \] gives \[ \cos( eta-\alpha)= rac{u_1v_1+u_2v_2}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}. \] Dot product For vectors in the plane, \[  oxed{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=u_1v_1+u_2v_2}. \] In three-dimensional space, \[  oxed{[u_1,u_2,u_3]\cdot[v_1,v_2,v_3]=u_1v_1+u_2v_2+u_3v_3}. \] Perpendicularity and angle \[  oxed{\mathbf u\perp\mathbf v\iff\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=0}. \] More generally, if \(	heta\) is the smaller angle between them, \[  oxed{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\cos	heta}. \] The dot product is positive for an acute angle, zero for a right angle, and negative for an obtuse angle. Example For \(\mathbf u=[3,2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-2,3]\) , \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=3(-2)+2(3)=0. \] Therefore \(\cos	heta=0\) , so the vectors are perpendicular. Summary The sine of the angle difference produces the parallelism test \(u_1v_2-u_2v_1=0\) . The cosine of the same difference produces the perpendicularity test \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=0\) . Neither rule is imposed arbitrarily; both are consequences of the geometry of angle. The angle between two vectors ¶ Directed turn versus ordinary angle The difference of inclination angles \( eta-\alpha\) is a directed turn modulo a full turn. The ordinary angle between two nonzero vectors is the smaller, unoriented separation between their directions. Angle between vectors For nonzero vectors \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) , their angle is the unique number \(	heta\in[0,\pi]\) satisfying \[  oxed{\cos	heta= rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}}. \] The interval \([0,\pi]\) chooses the smaller unoriented angle. Directions with inclinations \(10^\circ\) and \(350^\circ\) are separated by \(20^\circ\) , not \(340^\circ\) . Principal relative positions \[ 	heta=0\iff	ext{same direction}, \qquad 	heta= rac\pi2\iff	ext{perpendicular}, \qquad 	heta=\pi\iff	ext{opposite directions}. \] The Cauchy--Schwarz inequality guarantees that \[ -1\leq rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}\leq1, \] so the angle is well defined. Its proof will emerge from orthogonal projection later in the lecture. Why the dot product can be expanded ¶ Before using the dot product in longer calculations, we verify its behaviour under addition and scalar multiplication. Algebraic properties of the dot product Let \(\mathbf u=[u_1,u_2]\) , \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) , and \(\mathbf w=[w_1,w_2]\) . Then \[  egin{aligned} (\mathbf u+\mathbf w)\cdot\mathbf v &=(u_1+w_1)v_1+(u_2+w_2)v_2\ &=(u_1v_1+u_2v_2)+(w_1v_1+w_2v_2)\ &=\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v+\mathbf w\cdot\mathbf v. \end{aligned} \] Similarly, \[ (\lambda\mathbf u)\cdot\mathbf v=\lambda(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v). \] Symmetry of multiplication of real numbers gives \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf u, \] and finally \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf u=u_1^2+u_2^2=\|\mathbf u\|^2. \] Dot-product properties \[  egin{aligned} (\mathbf u+\mathbf w)\cdot\mathbf v&=\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v+\mathbf w\cdot\mathbf v,\ (\lambda\mathbf u)\cdot\mathbf v&=\lambda(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v),\ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v&=\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf u. \end{aligned} \] \[  oxed{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf u=\|\mathbf u\|^2}. \] These properties justify every later expansion involving expressions such as \((\mathbf u-\mathbf v)\cdot(\mathbf u-\mathbf v)\) . The cosine theorem emerges immediately ¶ Draw \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) from a common point. The displacement from the endpoint of \(\mathbf v\) to the endpoint of \(\mathbf u\) is \(\mathbf u-\mathbf v\) . Thus the three vectors determine a triangle with side lengths \(\|\mathbf u\|\) , \(\|\mathbf v\|\) , and \(\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|\) . Expanding the third side \[  egin{aligned} \|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|^2 &=(\mathbf u-\mathbf v)\cdot(\mathbf u-\mathbf v)\ &=\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf u-\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v-\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf u+\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v\ &=\|\mathbf u\|^2+\|\mathbf v\|^2-2\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v\ &=\|\mathbf u\|^2+\|\mathbf v\|^2-2\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\cos	heta. \end{aligned} \] Cosine theorem If \(a=\|\mathbf u\|\) , \(b=\|\mathbf v\|\) , and \(c=\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|\) , then \[  oxed{c^2=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos	heta}. \] The cosine theorem is not inserted as an external formula. It is the expansion of the squared displacement between the endpoints of two arbitrary vectors. The triangle law is encoded in vector subtraction and the dot product. Projection is forced by the right-angle condition ¶ Let \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) . We seek a multiple \(\lambda\mathbf v\) such that the remainder \[ \mathbf u-\lambda\mathbf v \] is perpendicular to \(\mathbf v\) . Geometrically, this means decomposing \(\mathbf u\) into a component parallel to \(\mathbf v\) and a component perpendicular to \(\mathbf v\) : \[ \mathbf u=\lambda\mathbf v+(\mathbf u-\lambda\mathbf v). \] Orthogonal projection Move $\mathbf u$ and the direction $\mathbf v$. ⛶ The point on the line spanned by $\mathbf v$ is determined by the right-angle condition. Geometric requirement The foot \(H\) lies on the line spanned by \(\mathbf v\) . The vector from the origin to \(H\) is the parallel component \(\lambda\mathbf v\) , while the vector from \(H\) to the endpoint of \(\mathbf u\) is the perpendicular remainder. The right-angle condition determines \(\lambda\) . Solving the right-angle condition Perpendicularity requires \[ (\mathbf u-\lambda\mathbf v)\cdot\mathbf v=0. \] Expanding gives \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v-\lambda(\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v)=0, \] so \[ \lambda= rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}. \] Projection formula For \(\mathbf v
eq\mathbf0\) , \[  oxed{\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u= rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}\,\mathbf v}. \] The formula is the solution of a geometric requirement, not a rule to be memorised without explanation. Orthogonal decomposition and its consequences ¶ Orthogonal decomposition \[  oxed{\mathbf u=\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u+\left(\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf uight)}. \] The first term is parallel to \(\mathbf v\) and the second is perpendicular to \(\mathbf v\) . Pythagorean identity for components \[  oxed{\|\mathbf u\|^2=\left\|\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf uight\|^2+\left\|\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf uight\|^2}. \] Scalar component and vector projection ¶ Scalar and vector components The signed scalar component of \(\mathbf u\) in the direction of nonzero \(\mathbf v\) is \[  oxed{\operatorname{comp}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u= rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf v\|}}. \] The corresponding vector projection is \[  oxed{\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u=\operatorname{comp}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u rac{\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf v\|}}. \] The scalar component measures signed length along the chosen direction; multiplying by the unit vector turns that amount back into an actual displacement. The Cauchy--Schwarz inequality ¶ Derivation from projection The parallel component cannot be longer than the whole vector: \[ \left|\operatorname{comp}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf uight|\leq\|\mathbf u\|. \] Substituting the scalar-component formula gives \[  rac{|\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v|}{\|\mathbf v\|}\leq\|\mathbf u\|. \] Multiplying by \(\|\mathbf v\|>0\) yields \[  oxed{|\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v|\leq\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}. \] Consequently, for nonzero vectors, \[ -1\leq rac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}\leq1, \] so the angle formula using \(\arccos\) is meaningful. The triangle inequality ¶ Derivation from Cauchy--Schwarz Using Cauchy--Schwarz, \[  egin{aligned} \|\mathbf u+\mathbf v\|^2 &=\|\mathbf u\|^2+2\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v+\|\mathbf v\|^2\ &\leq\|\mathbf u\|^2+2\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|+\|\mathbf v\|^2\ &=(\|\mathbf u\|+\|\mathbf v\|)^2. \end{aligned} \] Both sides are nonnegative, so taking square roots gives \[  oxed{\|\mathbf u+\mathbf v\|\leq\|\mathbf u\|+\|\mathbf v\|}. \] The triangle inequality is the algebraic form of the Euclidean fact that the straight segment between two points is no longer than a route that changes direction at an intermediate point. One vector in a different basis ¶ A vector does not change when we describe it in another basis. Only the numbers used to reconstruct it from the chosen basis vectors change. One numerical example is enough to see the mechanism. Let \[ \mathbf b_1=[1,1],\qquad \mathbf b_2=[1,-1] \] and consider \[ \mathbf v=[4,2]. \] We seek numbers \(\alpha\) and \( eta\) such that \[ \mathbf v=\alpha\mathbf b_1+ eta\mathbf b_2. \] The same vector, two numerical descriptions $\mathbf v=3\mathbf b_1+\mathbf b_2$ ⛶ $\alpha$ = 3 $ eta$ = 1 The sliders show how the same vector is reconstructed from the two chosen basis directions. Solving for the basis coordinates Using the Cartesian coordinates of the basis vectors gives \[  egin{aligned} [4,2] &=\alpha[1,1]+ eta[1,-1]\ &=[\alpha+ eta,\alpha- eta]. \end{aligned} \] Therefore \[ \alpha+ eta=4, \qquad \alpha- eta=2. \] Solving this small system gives \[ \alpha=3, \qquad  eta=1. \] Hence \[  oxed{\mathbf v=3\mathbf b_1+\mathbf b_2} \] and the coordinates of the same vector in the basis \(B=(\mathbf b_1,\mathbf b_2)\) are \[  oxed{[\mathbf v]_B=[3,1]}. \] What changes and what does not The Cartesian coordinates \([4,2]\) and the basis coordinates \([3,1]_B\) are two numerical descriptions of the same geometric vector. Changing the basis means solving for the coefficients in one concrete linear combination; no additional general machinery is introduced here. Exercises ¶ The first ten consolidate calculation and construction. The next ten require explanation, proof, comparison, or modelling. Download exercises as Markdown A. Elementary computational and graphical exercises ¶ Let \(A=(1,2)\) and \(B=(5,-1)\) . Compute \([\overrightarrow{AB}]\) . Draw equal representatives beginning at \(O=(0,0)\) and \(P=(-2,3)\) , and find their endpoints. For \(\mathbf u=[3,-2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-1,4]\) , compute \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) , \(\mathbf u-\mathbf v\) , \(2\mathbf u\) , and \(-3\mathbf v\) . Draw the parallelogram for \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) . For \(\mathbf u=[1,2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[2,-1]\) , compute \(2\mathbf u-3\mathbf v\) and reproduce it head to tail. Compute the lengths and unit vectors for \([3,4]\) , \([-5,12]\) , and \([2,-2]\) . Classify the pairs \([2,1],[6,3]\) ; \([2,1],[1,-2]\) ; \([3,-1],[2,5]\) as parallel, perpendicular, or neither. Find the angles between \([1,0]\) and \([1,1]\) , and between \([1,1]\) and \([-1,1]\) . For \(\mathbf u=[3,1]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-1,2]\) , compute \(\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|\) directly and from the cosine theorem. Project \([4,3]\) onto \([1,0]\) and onto \([1,1]\) . Verify that each remainder is perpendicular to the target direction. For \(\mathbf b_1=[1,1]\) , \(\mathbf b_2=[1,-1]\) , and \(\mathbf u=[4,2]\) , find \([\mathbf u]_B\) and reconstruct \(\mathbf u\) . Starting from \(P=(2,-1)\) , apply \([3,2]\) , \([-1,4]\) , and \(-2[3,2]\) . Find the endpoint in two ways. B. Development and reasoning exercises ¶ Explain with a parallelogram why translating an arrow without changing its directed change does not change the free vector. Give a numerical example. Draw \((\mathbf u+\mathbf v)+\mathbf w\) and \(\mathbf u+(\mathbf v+\mathbf w)\) for concrete vectors and explain geometrically why the endpoints coincide. Prove that two nonzero plane vectors are parallel exactly when one is a scalar multiple of the other. Discuss positive and negative multiples separately. Find equal-length vectors with \(\|\mathbf u+\mathbf v\|<\|\mathbf u\|\) and explain the cancellation geometrically. Derive \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=\|\mathbf u\|\|\mathbf v\|\cos	heta\) from the cosine theorem and interpret the sign of the dot product. Prove from the projection formula that \(\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u\) is perpendicular to \(\mathbf v\) . Explain why two nonparallel basis vectors give unique coefficients in \(\mathbf u=\alpha\mathbf b_1+ eta\mathbf b_2\) . Explain the type difference between \(P=(3,2)\) and \(\mathbf v=[3,2]\) using one location example and one displacement example. Find the standard and \(B\) -coordinates of \([6,2]\) for \(\mathbf b_1=[1,1]\) , \(\mathbf b_2=[1,-1]\) . Explain when a basis is well adapted. Refute the claim that every pair of nonzero vectors is a basis. Give one dependent and one independent example and interpret their linear combinations.
docs.title: Lecture 02 · Vectors, Bases, and Coordinate Systems

docs.text: A point of the Cartesian plane is an ordered pair of real numbers. Let \[ P=(p_1,p_2),\qquad Q=(q_1,q_2). \] Before vectors are introduced, we first define several arithmetic operations on such points. These definitions are possible because a point of the Cartesian plane is represented by an ordered pair of numbers.
docs.title: From Cartesian points to free vectors

docs.text: The point \[ O=(0,0) \] plays the role of zero. Addition of points is defined coordinatewise: \[ \boxed{P+Q=(p_1+q_1,\,p_2+q_2)}. \] The point opposite to \(P\) is \[ \boxed{-P=(-p_1,-p_2)}, \] so that \[ P+(-P)=O. \] Subtraction of points is defined by adding the opposite point: \[ \boxed{Q-P=Q+(-P)=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2)}. \] For a real number \(\lambda\) , multiplication of a point by a number is also defined coordinatewise: \[ \boxed{\lambda P=(\lambda p_1,\,\lambda p_2)}. \] At this stage these are arithmetic operations on ordered pairs. They are useful computational rules, but their geometric meaning has not yet been established. The first expression to acquire such a meaning will be the difference \(Q-P\) . Arithmetic on Cartesian points Move $P$ and $Q$, then compare the four coordinatewise operations. ⛶ Addition Opposite point Subtraction Scalar multiplication $\lambda$ = 1.5 The figure visualises arithmetic on ordered pairs. The interpretation of $Q-P$ as a directed displacement is introduced immediately afterwards.
docs.title: Arithmetic operations on Cartesian points

docs.text: The ordered pair \((P,Q)\) determines an arrow beginning at \(P\) and ending at \(Q\) . We denote it by \[ \overrightarrow{PQ} \] and call it an anchored vector . The order matters: \(P\) is its initial point and \(Q\) is its endpoint. An anchored vector and its coordinate changes The horizontal and vertical changes are $q_1-p_1$ and $q_2-p_2$. ⛶ The arrow begins at $P$ and ends at $Q$; its coordinates record the two coordinate changes. The point difference is \[ Q-P=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2). \] The two numbers \(q_1-p_1\) and \(q_2-p_2\) are, by definition, the coordinates of the anchored vector \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) . Round brackets remain reserved for Cartesian points. Its length is the distance between the two points: \[ \boxed{|\overrightarrow{PQ}|=d_E(P,Q)}. \] Reversing the order reverses the arrow and changes the signs of both coordinates. The vector \(\overrightarrow{QP}\) therefore has coordinates \[ p_1-q_1,\qquad p_2-q_2. \]
docs.title: An ordered pair of points determines an anchored vector

docs.text: We first define equality geometrically. Consider two anchored vectors \[ \overrightarrow{PQ}\qquad\text{and}\qquad\overrightarrow{P'Q'}. \] They are called equal when the midpoint of the pair \(P,Q'\) is also the midpoint of the pair \(Q,P'\) . Equivalently, the four points form a parallelogram in which the two arrows are opposite directed sides. The midpoint condition is \[ \frac12(P+Q')=\frac12(Q+P'). \] Rearranging this equality gives \[ Q-P=Q'-P'. \] We have therefore obtained the coordinate criterion for the geometric relation: \[ \boxed{\overrightarrow{PQ}=\overrightarrow{P'Q'}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad Q-P=Q'-P'}. \] Thus two anchored vectors are equal precisely when their corresponding coordinates are equal. Their initial points may be different.
docs.title: When do arrows drawn in different places represent the same vector?

docs.text: Equality divides all anchored vectors into disjoint classes. Each class contains all arrows representing one and the same directed change. Such a class is called a free vector . If \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) is one representative of a free vector \(\mathbf v\) , we write \[ \boxed{\mathbf v=[\overrightarrow{PQ}]}. \] Here the square brackets denote the equivalence class of all anchored vectors equal to \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) . If the common coordinates of these representatives are \(v_1,v_2\) , then the same free vector is written \[ \boxed{\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]}. \] Thus round brackets distinguish points from vectors: \[ V=(v_1,v_2)\qquad\text{is a point, whereas}\qquad \mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\qquad\text{is a free vector}. \] Since \[ Q-P=(q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2) \] is a Cartesian point, placing square brackets around this point means the free vector with the same coordinate entries: \[ \boxed{[\overrightarrow{PQ}]=[Q-P]=[q_1-p_1,\,q_2-p_2]}. \] The outer square brackets change the type of object: \((q_1-p_1,q_2-p_2)\) is a point, while \([q_1-p_1,q_2-p_2]\) is a free vector. A particularly convenient representative begins at the origin. If \[ O=(0,0),\qquad V=(v_1,v_2), \] then \[ \mathbf v=[\overrightarrow{OV}]=[V]=[v_1,v_2]. \] This does not identify the point \(V\) with the vector \(\mathbf v\) ; it gives a canonical representative of the free vector. Order of construction Only now, after free vectors have been constructed, do we define operations on them. Each operation will first be described using representatives and then written in coordinates.
docs.title: Free vectors

docs.text: Suppose that we begin at a point \(P\) , move to a point \(Q\) , and then move from \(Q\) to a point \(R\) . These are two consecutive geometric changes: \[ P\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow R. \] The first change is represented by the anchored vector \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) and the second by \(\overrightarrow{QR}\) . But the same journey also produces one direct change from the initial point \(P\) to the final point \(R\) , represented by \(\overrightarrow{PR}\) . The problem Given the two free vectors represented by \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) and \(\overrightarrow{QR}\) , construct one free vector that represents their combined effect. This geometric problem leads to the definition of vector addition. Two consecutive changes and one direct change Move $P$, $Q$, or $R$ and compare the route $P\to Q\to R$ with the direct change $P\to R$. ⛶ The two-step motion and the direct motion have the same initial and final points. The direct anchored vector is therefore the natural candidate for the combined free vector.
docs.title: Why an operation of addition is needed

docs.text: Let the free vector represented by \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) have coordinates \[ \mathbf a=[a_1,a_2], \] and let the free vector represented by \(\overrightarrow{QR}\) have coordinates \[ \mathbf b=[b_1,b_2]. \] Because \(\overrightarrow{PQ}\) has coordinate change \((a_1,a_2)\) , \[ Q=P+(a_1,a_2). \] Because \(\overrightarrow{QR}\) has coordinate change \((b_1,b_2)\) , \[ R=Q+(b_1,b_2). \] Now substitute the expression for the point \(Q\) into the formula for the point \(R\) : \[ \begin{aligned} R &=Q+(b_1,b_2)\\ &=\bigl(P+(a_1,a_2)\bigr)+(b_1,b_2). \end{aligned} \] At this point we are still using the coordinatewise addition of Cartesian points introduced earlier. By associativity, \[ R=P+\bigl((a_1,a_2)+(b_1,b_2)\bigr). \] Adding the two ordered pairs coordinatewise gives \[ (a_1,a_2)+(b_1,b_2)=(a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2), \] and therefore \[ \boxed{R=P+(a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2).} \] Hence the direct anchored vector \(\overrightarrow{PR}\) has coordinates \[ [\overrightarrow{PR}]=[a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2]. \] These coordinates depend only on the two free vectors \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\) , not on the particular starting point \(P\) . We therefore define \[ \boxed{\mathbf a+\mathbf b=[\overrightarrow{PR}]=[a_1+b_1,\,a_2+b_2].} \]
docs.title: From two successive displacements to vector addition

docs.text: The same sum can be constructed in the opposite order. Placing a representative of \(\mathbf b\) first and then one of \(\mathbf a\) gives the same endpoint. Hence \[ \boxed{\mathbf a+\mathbf b=\mathbf b+\mathbf a.} \]
docs.title: The parallelogram rule and commutativity

docs.text: For every vector \(\mathbf a=[a_1,a_2]\) there is an opposite vector \[ \boxed{-\mathbf a=[-a_1,-a_2]}, \] characterised by \[ \mathbf a+(-\mathbf a)=\mathbf0. \] Geometrically it has the same length and lies on the same direction line as \(\mathbf a\) , but its orientation is reversed.
docs.title: Opposite vectors

docs.text: Opposite vector and subtraction First reverse $\mathbf b$, then add $-\mathbf b$ to $\mathbf a$. ⛶ Construct $-\mathbf b$ Add $\mathbf a+(-\mathbf b)$ Check the missing vector The opposite vector reverses orientation without changing length. Subtraction is then ordinary vector addition with this reversed vector. Subtraction is defined by adding the opposite vector: \[ \boxed{\mathbf a-\mathbf b=\mathbf a+(-\mathbf b)=[a_1-b_1,\,a_2-b_2].} \] It is the missing vector in the equation \[ (\mathbf a-\mathbf b)+\mathbf b=\mathbf a. \] Points and vectors For any two points \(P\) and \(Q\) , \[ [\overrightarrow{PQ}]=[Q-P]. \] The subtraction is performed on their coordinate descriptions and the result is interpreted as the free vector represented by the arrow from \(P\) to \(Q\) .
docs.title: Subtraction as addition of the opposite vector

docs.text: Before multiplying a vector by a number, we first identify the geometric relation that this operation is meant to preserve. Parallel vectors from length equalities Two nonzero free vectors \(\mathbf a\) and \(\mathbf b\) have the same direction and the same orientation when placing them successively produces no shortening: \[ |\mathbf a+\mathbf b|=|\mathbf a|+|\mathbf b|. \] They have the same direction and opposite orientations when \[ |\mathbf a-\mathbf b|=|\mathbf a|+|\mathbf b|. \] In both cases the vectors are called parallel. For \(\mathbf a=[a_1,a_2]\) and \(\mathbf b=[b_1,b_2]\) , equality in the triangle inequality gives the coordinate criterion \[ \boxed{\mathbf a\parallel\mathbf b\iff \lambda\mathbf a=\mu\mathbf b} \] for some real numbers \(\lambda\) and \(\mu\) that are not both zero. In the plane this may also be written as \[ \boxed{a_1b_2-a_2b_1=0.} \] If \(\lambda\) and \(\mu\) have the same sign, the orientations agree; if their signs are opposite, the orientations are opposite.
docs.title: Parallel vectors, multiplication by a number, and linear combinations

docs.text: If \(t>0\) , then \(t\mathbf a\) is parallel to \(\mathbf a\) , has the same orientation, and \[ |t\mathbf a|=t|\mathbf a|. \] If \(t<0\) , the orientation is reversed and \[ |t\mathbf a|=|t|\,|\mathbf a|. \] For \(t=0\) , the result is the zero vector. In coordinates, \[ \boxed{t[a_1,a_2]=[ta_1,ta_2].} \] Scaling and combining vectors $2\mathbf u-\mathbf v$ ⛶ $a$ = 2 $b$ = -1 The two scaled displacements are added head to tail to form $a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v$. Scalar multiplication is compatible with addition: \[ \boxed{t(\mathbf a+\mathbf b)=t\mathbf a+t\mathbf b}, \qquad \boxed{(s+t)\mathbf a=s\mathbf a+t\mathbf a}. \] These identities follow from coordinatewise arithmetic, while geometrically they say that scaling commutes with combining directed changes.
docs.title: Multiplication by a real number

docs.text: Linear combination Given vectors \(\mathbf u,\mathbf v\) and real numbers \(a,b\) , the vector \[ \boxed{a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v} \] is called a linear combination of \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) . First scale \(\mathbf u\) by \(a\) and \(\mathbf v\) by \(b\) , then add the resulting displacements. A linear combination is therefore not a new operation. One direction versus two directions A single nonzero vector generates only the line \[ \{\lambda\mathbf v:\lambda\in\mathbb R\}. \] Two parallel vectors still generate one line. Two nonparallel vectors provide two independent directions and can generate the whole plane. Why nonparallel directions reach the plane If \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) are parallel, every multiple of either vector lies on the same line, so every sum \(a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v\) remains on that line. If they are not parallel, the parallelogram construction decomposes any target displacement into one component along each direction. The two coefficients record those two signed motions. Basis theorem in the plane Two nonparallel vectors \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) determine every vector in the plane uniquely in the form \[ \boxed{\mathbf w=a\mathbf u+b\mathbf v}. \] A basis is precisely such a pair of independent directions, and basis coordinates are the coefficients in this reconstruction.
docs.title: Linear combinations: what addition and scaling can build

docs.text: The coordinate pair of a vector records two changes, but it does not immediately answer two geometric questions: Two questions carried by every nonzero vector How long is the displacement? In which direction does it point? Let \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) be drawn from the origin. Its endpoint, together with its horizontal and vertical coordinate changes, forms a right triangle. The legs have lengths \(|v_1|\) and \(|v_2|\) , while the vector itself is the hypotenuse. A vector as a right triangle $\mathbf v=[4,3]$ ⛶ The vector, its two coordinate changes, and its inclination are displayed simultaneously. Length from the Pythagorean theorem \[ \|\mathbf v\|^2=v_1^2+v_2^2. \] The length is nonnegative, so \[ \boxed{\|\mathbf v\|=\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}}. \] Euclidean norm The Euclidean length, or norm, of \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) is \[ \boxed{\|\mathbf v\|=\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2}}. \] The double bars distinguish the vector from the number measuring its length. Basic consequences \[ \boxed{\|\mathbf0\|=0}, \qquad \boxed{\mathbf v\neq\mathbf0\implies\|\mathbf v\|>0}. \] For points \(A\) and \(B\) , \[ \boxed{d_E(A,B)=\|B-A\|}. \] The vector \(B-A\) records how to move from \(A\) to \(B\) , while its norm records the Euclidean distance travelled. The same object therefore carries both directional and metric information.
docs.title: How much motion and in which direction?

docs.text: Assume \(\mathbf v\neq\mathbf0\) and let \(\alpha\) be its angle of inclination measured from the positive \(x\) -axis. Recovering the coordinates The right triangle gives \[ \cos\alpha=\frac{v_1}{\|\mathbf v\|}, \qquad \sin\alpha=\frac{v_2}{\|\mathbf v\|}. \] Therefore \[ v_1=\|\mathbf v\|\cos\alpha, \qquad v_2=\|\mathbf v\|\sin\alpha. \] Magnitude-direction decomposition Every nonzero vector admits the decomposition \[ \boxed{\mathbf v=\|\mathbf v\|[\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]}. \] The vector \([\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]\) has length one. The two pieces of a vector \(\|\mathbf v\|\) says how much displacement occurs; \([\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha]\) says in which direction it occurs. Thus every nonzero vector is a positive magnitude multiplied by a unit direction.
docs.title: Separating magnitude from direction

docs.text: Take two arbitrary nonzero vectors \[ \mathbf u=[u_1,u_2],\qquad \mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]. \] Let their angles of inclination be \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) . Then \[ \mathbf u=\|\mathbf u\|[\cos\alpha,\sin\alpha], \qquad \mathbf v=\|\mathbf v\|[\cos\beta,\sin\beta]. \] One comparison controls both tests The directed turn from \(\mathbf u\) to \(\mathbf v\) is \(\beta-\alpha\) . Its sine detects parallel directions, while its cosine detects perpendicular directions. Two vectors, two coordinate tests Move the endpoints of $\mathbf u$ and $\mathbf v$. ⛶ The same angle difference produces both coordinate tests.
docs.title: One angle calculation, two geometric tests

docs.text:
Parallelism from \(\sin( eta-\alpha)\) Two nonzero vectors determine the same unoriented direction precisely when \[  eta-\alpha\equiv0\pmod\pi, \] equivalently when \(\sin( eta-\alpha)=0\) . Using \[ \sin( eta-\alpha)=\sin eta\cos\alpha-\cos eta\sin\alpha \] and substituting the coordinate expressions for the four trigonometric terms gives \[ \sin( eta-\alpha)= rac{u_1v_2-u_2v_1}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}. \] The denominator is nonzero, so the sine vanishes exactly when the numerator vanishes. Parallelism test \[  oxed{\mathbf u\parallel\mathbf v\iff u_1v_2-u_2v_1=0}. \] Equivalently, \[  oxed{\mathbf u\parallel\mathbf v\iff \mathbf u=\lambda\mathbf v	ext{ for some }\lambda
eq0}. \] Why the determinant condition gives a scalar multiple If \(v_1
eq0\) , set \(\lambda=u_1/v_1\) . The equality \(u_1v_2-u_2v_1=0\) then gives \(u_2=\lambda v_2\) , hence \(\mathbf u=\lambda\mathbf v\) . If \(v_1=0\) , then \(v_2
eq0\) and the same argument uses \(\lambda=u_2/v_2\) . The quantity \(u_1v_2-u_2v_1\) measures whether the two coordinate directions span genuine area. It is zero exactly when both vectors lie on one line.
docs.title: Parallel directions arise from the sine of the angle difference

docs.text: Perpendicularity from \(\cos(\beta-\alpha)\) The vectors are perpendicular precisely when \[ \beta-\alpha\equiv\frac\pi2\pmod\pi, \] equivalently when \(\cos(\beta-\alpha)=0\) . The identity \[ \cos(\beta-\alpha)=\cos\beta\cos\alpha+\sin\beta\sin\alpha \] gives \[ \cos(\beta-\alpha)=\frac{u_1v_1+u_2v_2}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}. \] Dot product For vectors in the plane, \[ \boxed{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=u_1v_1+u_2v_2}. \] In three-dimensional space, \[ \boxed{[u_1,u_2,u_3]\cdot[v_1,v_2,v_3]=u_1v_1+u_2v_2+u_3v_3}. \] Perpendicularity and angle \[ \boxed{\mathbf u\perp\mathbf v\iff\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=0}. \] More generally, if \(\theta\) is the smaller angle between them, \[ \boxed{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\cos\theta}. \] The dot product is positive for an acute angle, zero for a right angle, and negative for an obtuse angle. Example For \(\mathbf u=[3,2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-2,3]\) , \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=3(-2)+2(3)=0. \] Therefore \(\cos\theta=0\) , so the vectors are perpendicular. Summary The sine of the angle difference produces the parallelism test \(u_1v_2-u_2v_1=0\) . The cosine of the same difference produces the perpendicularity test \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=0\) . Neither rule is imposed arbitrarily; both are consequences of the geometry of angle.
docs.title: The cosine of the same difference reveals perpendicularity

docs.text: Directed turn versus ordinary angle The difference of inclination angles \(\beta-\alpha\) is a directed turn modulo a full turn. The ordinary angle between two nonzero vectors is the smaller, unoriented separation between their directions. Angle between vectors For nonzero vectors \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) , their angle is the unique number \(\theta\in[0,\pi]\) satisfying \[ \boxed{\cos\theta=\frac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}}. \] The interval \([0,\pi]\) chooses the smaller unoriented angle. Directions with inclinations \(10^\circ\) and \(350^\circ\) are separated by \(20^\circ\) , not \(340^\circ\) . Principal relative positions \[ \theta=0\iff\text{same direction}, \qquad \theta=\frac\pi2\iff\text{perpendicular}, \qquad \theta=\pi\iff\text{opposite directions}. \] The Cauchy--Schwarz inequality guarantees that \[ -1\leq\frac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}\leq1, \] so the angle is well defined. Its proof will emerge from orthogonal projection later in the lecture.
docs.title: The angle between two vectors

docs.text: Before using the dot product in longer calculations, we verify its behaviour under addition and scalar multiplication. Algebraic properties of the dot product Let \(\mathbf u=[u_1,u_2]\) , \(\mathbf v=[v_1,v_2]\) , and \(\mathbf w=[w_1,w_2]\) . Then \[ \begin{aligned} (\mathbf u+\mathbf w)\cdot\mathbf v &=(u_1+w_1)v_1+(u_2+w_2)v_2\\ &=(u_1v_1+u_2v_2)+(w_1v_1+w_2v_2)\\ &=\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v+\mathbf w\cdot\mathbf v. \end{aligned} \] Similarly, \[ (\lambda\mathbf u)\cdot\mathbf v=\lambda(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v). \] Symmetry of multiplication of real numbers gives \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf u, \] and finally \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf u=u_1^2+u_2^2=\|\mathbf u\|^2. \] Dot-product properties \[ \begin{aligned} (\mathbf u+\mathbf w)\cdot\mathbf v&=\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v+\mathbf w\cdot\mathbf v,\\ (\lambda\mathbf u)\cdot\mathbf v&=\lambda(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v),\\ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v&=\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf u. \end{aligned} \] \[ \boxed{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf u=\|\mathbf u\|^2}. \] These properties justify every later expansion involving expressions such as \((\mathbf u-\mathbf v)\cdot(\mathbf u-\mathbf v)\) .
docs.title: Why the dot product can be expanded

docs.text: Draw \(\mathbf u\) and \(\mathbf v\) from a common point. The displacement from the endpoint of \(\mathbf v\) to the endpoint of \(\mathbf u\) is \(\mathbf u-\mathbf v\) . Thus the three vectors determine a triangle with side lengths \(\|\mathbf u\|\) , \(\|\mathbf v\|\) , and \(\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|\) . Expanding the third side \[ \begin{aligned} \|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|^2 &=(\mathbf u-\mathbf v)\cdot(\mathbf u-\mathbf v)\\ &=\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf u-\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v-\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf u+\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v\\ &=\|\mathbf u\|^2+\|\mathbf v\|^2-2\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v\\ &=\|\mathbf u\|^2+\|\mathbf v\|^2-2\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\cos\theta. \end{aligned} \] Cosine theorem If \(a=\|\mathbf u\|\) , \(b=\|\mathbf v\|\) , and \(c=\|\mathbf u-\mathbf v\|\) , then \[ \boxed{c^2=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos\theta}. \] The cosine theorem is not inserted as an external formula. It is the expansion of the squared displacement between the endpoints of two arbitrary vectors. The triangle law is encoded in vector subtraction and the dot product.
docs.title: The cosine theorem emerges immediately

docs.text: Let \(\mathbf v\neq\mathbf0\) . We seek a multiple \(\lambda\mathbf v\) such that the remainder \[ \mathbf u-\lambda\mathbf v \] is perpendicular to \(\mathbf v\) . Geometrically, this means decomposing \(\mathbf u\) into a component parallel to \(\mathbf v\) and a component perpendicular to \(\mathbf v\) : \[ \mathbf u=\lambda\mathbf v+(\mathbf u-\lambda\mathbf v). \] Orthogonal projection Move $\mathbf u$ and the direction $\mathbf v$. ⛶ The point on the line spanned by $\mathbf v$ is determined by the right-angle condition. Geometric requirement The foot \(H\) lies on the line spanned by \(\mathbf v\) . The vector from the origin to \(H\) is the parallel component \(\lambda\mathbf v\) , while the vector from \(H\) to the endpoint of \(\mathbf u\) is the perpendicular remainder. The right-angle condition determines \(\lambda\) . Solving the right-angle condition Perpendicularity requires \[ (\mathbf u-\lambda\mathbf v)\cdot\mathbf v=0. \] Expanding gives \[ \mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v-\lambda(\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v)=0, \] so \[ \lambda=\frac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}. \] Projection formula For \(\mathbf v\neq\mathbf0\) , \[ \boxed{\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u=\frac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}\,\mathbf v}. \] The formula is the solution of a geometric requirement, not a rule to be memorised without explanation.
docs.title: Projection is forced by the right-angle condition

docs.text: Orthogonal decomposition \[ \boxed{\mathbf u=\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u+\left(\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u\right)}. \] The first term is parallel to \(\mathbf v\) and the second is perpendicular to \(\mathbf v\) . Pythagorean identity for components \[ \boxed{\|\mathbf u\|^2=\left\|\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u\right\|^2+\left\|\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u\right\|^2}. \]
docs.title: Orthogonal decomposition and its consequences

docs.text: Scalar and vector components The signed scalar component of \(\mathbf u\) in the direction of nonzero \(\mathbf v\) is \[ \boxed{\operatorname{comp}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u=\frac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf v\|}}. \] The corresponding vector projection is \[ \boxed{\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u=\operatorname{comp}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u\frac{\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf v\|}}. \] The scalar component measures signed length along the chosen direction; multiplying by the unit vector turns that amount back into an actual displacement.
docs.title: Scalar component and vector projection

docs.text: Derivation from projection The parallel component cannot be longer than the whole vector: \[ \left|\operatorname{comp}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u\right|\leq\|\mathbf u\|. \] Substituting the scalar-component formula gives \[ \frac{|\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v|}{\|\mathbf v\|}\leq\|\mathbf u\|. \] Multiplying by \(\|\mathbf v\|>0\) yields \[ \boxed{|\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v|\leq\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}. \] Consequently, for nonzero vectors, \[ -1\leq\frac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|}\leq1, \] so the angle formula using \(\arccos\) is meaningful.
docs.title: The Cauchy--Schwarz inequality

docs.text: Derivation from Cauchy--Schwarz Using Cauchy--Schwarz, \[ \begin{aligned} \|\mathbf u+\mathbf v\|^2 &=\|\mathbf u\|^2+2\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v+\|\mathbf v\|^2\\ &\leq\|\mathbf u\|^2+2\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|+\|\mathbf v\|^2\\ &=(\|\mathbf u\|+\|\mathbf v\|)^2. \end{aligned} \] Both sides are nonnegative, so taking square roots gives \[ \boxed{\|\mathbf u+\mathbf v\|\leq\|\mathbf u\|+\|\mathbf v\|}. \] The triangle inequality is the algebraic form of the Euclidean fact that the straight segment between two points is no longer than a route that changes direction at an intermediate point.
docs.title: The triangle inequality

docs.text: A vector does not change when we describe it in another basis. Only the numbers used to reconstruct it from the chosen basis vectors change. One numerical example is enough to see the mechanism. Let \[ \mathbf b_1=[1,1],\qquad \mathbf b_2=[1,-1] \] and consider \[ \mathbf v=[4,2]. \] We seek numbers \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) such that \[ \mathbf v=\alpha\mathbf b_1+\beta\mathbf b_2. \] The same vector, two numerical descriptions $\mathbf v=3\mathbf b_1+\mathbf b_2$ ⛶ $\alpha$ = 3 $\beta$ = 1 The sliders show how the same vector is reconstructed from the two chosen basis directions. Solving for the basis coordinates Using the Cartesian coordinates of the basis vectors gives \[ \begin{aligned} [4,2] &=\alpha[1,1]+\beta[1,-1]\\ &=[\alpha+\beta,\alpha-\beta]. \end{aligned} \] Therefore \[ \alpha+\beta=4, \qquad \alpha-\beta=2. \] Solving this small system gives \[ \alpha=3, \qquad \beta=1. \] Hence \[ \boxed{\mathbf v=3\mathbf b_1+\mathbf b_2} \] and the coordinates of the same vector in the basis \(B=(\mathbf b_1,\mathbf b_2)\) are \[ \boxed{[\mathbf v]_B=[3,1]}. \] What changes and what does not The Cartesian coordinates \([4,2]\) and the basis coordinates \([3,1]_B\) are two numerical descriptions of the same geometric vector. Changing the basis means solving for the coefficients in one concrete linear combination; no additional general machinery is introduced here.
docs.title: One vector in a different basis

For \(\mathbf u=[3,-2]\) and \(\mathbf v=[-1,4]\) , compute \(\mathbf u+\mathbf v\) , \(\mathbf u-\mathbf v\) , \(2\mathbf u\) , and \(-3\mathbf v\) . Compute the lengths and unit vectors for \([3,4]\) , \([-5,12]\) , and \([2,-2]\) . Classify the pairs \([2,1],[6,3]\) ; \([2,1],[1,-2]\) ; \([3,-1],[2,5]\) as parallel, perpendicular, or neither. Draw \((\mathbf u+\mathbf v)+\mathbf w\) and \(\mathbf u+(\mathbf v+\mathbf w)\) for concrete vectors and explain geometrically why the endpoints coincide. Prove that two nonzero plane vectors are parallel exactly when one is a scalar multiple of the other. Discuss positive and negative multiples separately. Compute the free vector \([\overrightarrow{AB}]\) . Draw equal anchored representatives beginning at \(O=(0,0)\) and at \(P=(-2,3)\) , and write the endpoint of each representative. Development and reasoning exercises ¶ Two anchored vectors are declared equal when they represent the same translation. Explain, using a parallelogram construction, why moving an arrow without changing its length and direction does not change the free vector. Give one numerical example with two different initial points. Use three concrete vectors to draw both \((\mathbf u+\mathbf v)+\mathbf w\) and \(\mathbf u+(\mathbf v+\mathbf w)\) . Starting from the cosine theorem for the triangle formed by \(\mathbf u\) , \(\mathbf v\) , and \(\mathbf u-\mathbf v\) , derive \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=\|\mathbf u\|\,\|\mathbf v\|\cos\theta\) . Explain why the sign of the dot product distinguishes acute, right, and obtuse angles. Let \(\mathbf r=\mathbf u-\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u\) . Prove directly from the projection formula that \(\mathbf r\cdot\mathbf v=0\) . Then interpret the result geometrically. Suppose \(\mathbf b_1\) and \(\mathbf b_2\) are nonparallel. Explain why a vector cannot have two different representations \(\mathbf u=\alpha\mathbf b_1+\beta\mathbf b_2=\alpha'\mathbf b_1+\beta'\mathbf b_2\) . Reduce the question to a linear combination equal to the zero vector. Explain the difference between the point \(P=(3,2)\) and the vector \(\mathbf v=[3,2]\) . Construct one situation in which the same ordered pair refers to a location and another in which it refers to a displacement. The vector \(\mathbf u=[6,2]\) is awkward in the standard basis but simple in the basis \(\mathbf b_1=[1,1]\) , \(\mathbf b_2=[1,-1]\) . Find its coordinates in both bases and explain what it means for one basis to be better adapted to a problem. A student claims that every pair of nonzero vectors forms a basis of the plane. Give one counterexample and one valid example. For each pair, explain geometrically whether its linear combinations fill a line or the whole plane.
